title: Week 9: Introduction to Modal Logics — Kripke Semantics and Model-Checking

questions.text: Why is classical propositional logic insufficient for reasoning about modalities like 'possibly' and 'necessarily'? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Classical logic operates in a single static world where every proposition is simply true or false
Classical logic cannot express words like 'possibly', 'necessarily', 'I know that', 'it will be the case that'
Classical logic has too many connectives and needs to be simplified
Modal operators are non-truth-functional: $\Diamond F$'s value is not determined solely by $F$'s value

explanations.text: Classical propositional logic gives a fixed, here-and-now picture with no notion of possible worlds.
explanations.notes_quote: Classical propositional logic operates in a single, static world where every proposition is simply true or false

explanations.text: Modalities change the mode of truth and cannot be expressed with classical connectives.
explanations.notes_quote: natural language reasoning often involves modalities — words like 'possibly', 'necessarily', 'I know that', 'I believe that', 'it will be the case that'

explanations.text: The motivation is not about too many connectives but about the inability to express modalities.
Modal operators are non-truth-functional: knowing $F$ is true/false does not determine $\Diamond F$.
explanations.notes_quote: Modal operators are non-truth-functional: the truth value of $\Diamond F$ is not determined solely by the truth value of $F$

questions.source_ref: Section 9.1: Why truth tables cannot work
questions.text: There are exactly four possible truth tables for a unary connective on $\{0,1\}$. Which of the following correctly describes why none of them captures 'possibly'? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Op$_3$ (identity) only allows what is currently true to be possible, ignoring other worlds
Op$_4$ (always true) would make everything possible, even contradictions
Op$_1$ (always false) would make nothing possible
Op$_2$ (negation) would make something possible iff it is currently false, which is wrong
All four truth tables fail to capture 'possibly' because possibility depends on what happens in other worlds

explanations.text: Op$_3$ is the identity: $\text{Op}_3(F) = F$, meaning only what is true is possible — ignoring accessible worlds.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{Op}_3$: identity — only what is true is possible

explanations.text: Op$_4$ outputs 1 always: everything is possible, even when nothing is accessible.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{Op}_4$: 'true' operator — nothing is impossible

explanations.text: Op$_1$ outputs 0 always: nothing is possible, which is absurd.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{Op}_1$: 'false' operator — nothing is possible

explanations.text: Op$_2$ is negation: $F$ is possible iff $F$ is currently false — this is not the intended meaning of possibility.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{Op}_2$: negation — not useful

explanations.text: Possibility is relative to accessible worlds, a relational notion that truth tables cannot express.
explanations.notes_quote: None of these satisfactorily captures possibility/necessity. Hence modalities require a richer semantic framework.

questions.text: The set $\mathit{Fml}^\Box$ of well-formed modal formulas is defined inductively. Which of the following are well-formed modal formulas? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\Box p$
$\Diamond\Box p$
$\Box(p \to q)$
$\Diamond p \to \Box q$
$p \Box q$ (p and necessarily q)

explanations.text: $\Box p$ is well-formed: $p \in \mathit{Fml}^\Box$ and $\Box F \in \mathit{Fml}^\Box$ for any $F$.
explanations.notes_quote: Examples of modal formulas: $\Box p$, $\Diamond q$, $\Box(p \to q)$, $\Diamond \Box p$, $\Box \Diamond \neg p$, $\Diamond p \to \Box q$

explanations.text: $\Diamond\Box p$ is well-formed: $\Box p \in \mathit{Fml}^\Box$ and $\Diamond$ applied to it gives another formula.
explanations.notes_quote: Examples of modal formulas: $\Diamond \Box p$

explanations.text: $p \to q$ is a modal formula; applying $\Box$ gives $\Box(p \to q)$.
explanations.notes_quote: Examples of modal formulas: $\Box(p \to q)$

explanations.text: $\Diamond p$ and $\Box q$ are modal formulas; combining with $\to$ gives another.
explanations.notes_quote: Examples of modal formulas: $\Diamond p \to \Box q$

explanations.text: $p \Box q$ is not syntactically well-formed — $\Box$ is a unary prefix operator, not a binary infix one.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the relationship between $\mathit{Fml}$ (classical propositional formulas) and $\mathit{Fml}^\Box$ (modal formulas)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\mathit{Fml} \subseteq \mathit{Fml}^\Box$: every classical propositional formula is a modal formula
$\mathit{Fml}^\Box \subseteq \mathit{Fml}$: every modal formula is a classical formula
Modal formulas extend propositional formulas by adding $\Box F$ and $\Diamond F$ as new constructs
$\Box$ and $\Diamond$ are the two new unary connectives added to propositional logic

explanations.text: Since the base case and classical connectives are all included in $\mathit{Fml}^\Box$, every classical formula is also modal.
explanations.notes_quote: classical propositional formulas are a subset: $\mathit{Fml} \subseteq \mathit{Fml}^\Box$

explanations.text: $\mathit{Fml}^\Box$ is strictly larger; $\Box p$ is a modal formula but not a classical one.
Modal formulas add $\Box F$ and $\Diamond F$ to the inductive grammar.
explanations.notes_quote: If $F, G \in \mathit{Fml}^\Box$ then so are: $\neg F$, $(F \wedge G)$, $(F \vee G)$, $(F \to G)$, $\Box F$, $\Diamond F$

explanations.text: $\Box$ ('necessarily') and $\Diamond$ ('possibly') are the two new unary modal operators.
explanations.notes_quote: two new unary connectives called modal operators: $\Diamond F$ ('$F$ is possibly true') and $\Box F$ ('$F$ is necessarily true')

questions.text: A Kripke model $\mathfrak{M} = \langle W, R, V \rangle$ is defined with three components. Which of the following correctly describe these components? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $W$ is a non-empty set of possible worlds
$R \subseteq W \times W$ is a binary accessibility relation on $W$
$V: W \times \text{Prop} \to \{0,1\}$ assigns a Boolean value to each world-proposition pair
$W$ must be a finite set
The Kripke frame is the pair $\mathfrak{F} = \langle W, R \rangle$ (without the valuation)

explanations.text: $W$ is the non-empty set of possible worlds (vertices of the directed graph).
explanations.notes_quote: $W$ is a non-empty set of possible worlds (vertices)

explanations.text: $R \subseteq W \times W$ represents directed accessibility between worlds.
explanations.notes_quote: $R \subseteq W \times W$ is a binary accessibility relation on $W$ (directed edges between worlds)

explanations.text: $V$ assigns to each world $w$ and proposition $p$ a truth value 0 or 1.
explanations.notes_quote: $V : W \times \text{Prop} \to \{0, 1\}$ is a propositional valuation

explanations.text: $W$ need not be finite; the definition only requires it to be non-empty.
The Kripke frame is $\langle W, R \rangle$ — the model without the valuation.
explanations.notes_quote: The pair $\mathfrak{F} = \langle W, R \rangle$ is the Kripke frame underlying $\mathfrak{M}$.

questions.text: The satisfaction relation $\mathfrak{M}, w \models F$ is defined for modal formulas. Which of the following correctly state the clauses for $\Box$ and $\Diamond$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\mathfrak{M}, w \models \Diamond F$ iff there exists $u$ with $(w,u) \in R$ and $\mathfrak{M}, u \models F$
$\mathfrak{M}, w \models \Box F$ iff for all $u$ with $(w,u) \in R$, $\mathfrak{M}, u \models F$
$\mathfrak{M}, w \models \Box F$ iff $\mathfrak{M}, w \models F$ (Box is the identity)
If $w$ has no successors in $R$, then $\Box F$ is vacuously true at $w$
If $w$ has no successors in $R$, then $\Diamond F$ is vacuously false at $w$

explanations.text: $\Diamond F$ is true at $w$ if $F$ holds at some accessible world.
explanations.notes_quote: $\mathfrak{M}, w \models \Diamond F \iff \exists u \in W;\ (w, u) \in R \text{ and } \mathfrak{M}, u \models F$

explanations.text: $\Box F$ is true at $w$ if $F$ holds at all accessible worlds.
explanations.notes_quote: $\mathfrak{M}, w \models \Box F \iff \forall u \in W;\ (w, u) \in R \text{ implies } \mathfrak{M}, u \models F$

explanations.text: $\Box F$ looks at all successors of $w$, not at $w$ itself (unless the frame is reflexive).
With no successors, the universal quantification in $\Box F$ is vacuously true.
explanations.notes_quote: If $w$ has no successors (no $(w, u) \in R$), then $\Box F$ is vacuously true

explanations.text: With no successors, the existential in $\Diamond F$ cannot be satisfied.
explanations.notes_quote: If $w$ has no successors (no $(w, u) \in R$), then $\Diamond F$ is vacuously false at $w$.

questions.text: The notation $W(F)$ is used in model-checking. Which of the following correctly describe it and its use? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $W(F) = \{w \in W : \mathfrak{M}, w \models F\}$ is the set of worlds where $F$ holds
$W(\neg F) = W \setminus W(F)$
$W(F \wedge G) = W(F) \cap W(G)$
$W(F \vee G) = W(F) \setminus W(G)$
$W(\Diamond F) = \{w \in W : \exists v \in W(F)$ such that $(w,v) \in R\}$

explanations.text: $W(F)$ is defined as the extension of $F$ — the worlds satisfying $F$.
explanations.notes_quote: $W(F) = \{w \in W : V(w, F) = 1\}$ for the set of worlds where $F$ is satisfied

explanations.text: Negation flips membership: $W(\neg F)$ contains exactly those worlds not in $W(F)$.
explanations.notes_quote: $W(\neg F) = W \setminus W(F)$

explanations.text: Conjunction corresponds to intersection of world sets.
explanations.notes_quote: $W(F \wedge G) = W(F) \cap W(G)$

explanations.text: $W(F \vee G) = W(F) \cup W(G)$, not set difference.
explanations.notes_quote: $W(F \vee G) = W(F) \cup W(G)$

explanations.text: $W(\Diamond F)$ is the set of worlds that can see some world in $W(F)$.
explanations.notes_quote: $W(\Diamond F) = \{w \in W : (w, v) \in R \text{ for some } v \in W(F)\}$

questions.text: There are different levels in the validity hierarchy for modal formulas. Which of the following correctly describe these levels? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\mathfrak{M}, w \models F$: $F$ is satisfied at a specific world $w$ in model $\mathfrak{M}$
$\mathfrak{M} \models F$: $F$ is satisfied at every world in model $\mathfrak{M}$
$\mathfrak{F} \models F$: $F$ is satisfied in every model built on frame $\mathfrak{F}$
Validity over a frame implies validity over any model on that frame
Validity at a world implies validity over the model

explanations.text: This is the most basic: satisfiability at a specific world.
explanations.notes_quote: $\mathfrak{M}, w \models F$: fix model and world; existential over nothing

explanations.text: Model validity quantifies universally over worlds in the model.
explanations.notes_quote: $F$ is valid over the model $\mathfrak{M}$ if $\mathfrak{M}, w \models F$ for all $w \in W$

explanations.text: Frame validity quantifies over all worlds and all valuations on the frame.
explanations.notes_quote: $F$ is valid over the frame $\mathfrak{F} = (W, R)$ if $\mathfrak{M} \models F$ for every Kripke model $\mathfrak{M}$ with underlying frame $\mathfrak{F}$

explanations.text: Frame validity is stronger: it holds for every choice of valuation, so any model satisfies it.
Validity at a single world does not imply validity over the entire model; other worlds may not satisfy $F$.

questions.source_ref: Section 9.3.5: The Minimal Modal Logic K
questions.text: The minimal modal logic $\mathbf{K}$ is defined as the set of all modal formulas valid in every Kripke frame. Which of the following statements about $\mathbf{K}$ are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\mathbf{K} = \{F \in \mathit{Fml}^\Box : \mathfrak{F} \models F \text{ for all frames } \mathfrak{F}\}$
The Completeness Theorem states $\Gamma \models^{\mathbf{K}} F$ iff $\Gamma \vdash^{\mathbf{K}} F$
Axiom $A_K$: $\Box(p \to q) \to (\Box p \to \Box q)$ is valid in all Kripke frames
$\mathbf{K}$ is valid only in reflexive Kripke frames

explanations.text: This is the definition of $\mathbf{K}$ as the set of universal validities.
explanations.notes_quote: $\mathbf{K} = \{F \in \mathit{Fml}^\Box : \top \models^{\mathbf{K}} F\} = \{F : \mathfrak{F} \models F \text{ for all } \mathfrak{F}\}$

explanations.text: The Completeness Theorem for $\mathbf{K}$ equates semantic consequence with provability.
explanations.notes_quote: Theorem 9.6 (Completeness of K): $\Gamma \models^{\mathbf{K}} F \iff \Gamma \vdash^{\mathbf{k}} F$

explanations.text: Axiom $A_K = \Box(p \to q) \to (\Box p \to \Box q)$ is valid in every Kripke frame.
explanations.notes_quote: Important tautology (Axiom K): $\Box(p \to q) \to (\Box p \to \Box q)$. This is valid in all Kripke frames.

explanations.text: $\mathbf{K}$ is valid in all Kripke frames (not just reflexive ones). Reflexive frames correspond to the stronger logic $\mathbf{T}$.

questions.text: The axiom $A_T = \Box p \to p$ ('if $p$ is necessary then $p$ is true') and reflexive frames. Which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $A_T$ is not valid in all Kripke frames: a frame with $W=\{a\}$, $R=\varnothing$, $V(a,p)=0$ is a counterexample
$A_T$ is valid in all reflexive frames
Proof of validity on reflexive frames: if $\mathfrak{M},w \models \Box p$ then since $(w,w) \in R$, we get $\mathfrak{M},w \models p$
$A_T$ is valid in all transitive frames as well as reflexive frames

explanations.text: With no successors and $V(a,p)=0$: $\Box p$ is vacuously true but $p$ is false, violating $A_T$.
explanations.notes_quote: Not valid in all frames: Take $W = \{a\}$, $R = \emptyset$, $V(a,p)=0$. Then $\Box p$ is vacuously true but $p$ is false, so $\mathfrak{M}, a \not\models A_T$.

explanations.text: $A_T$ characterises exactly the class of reflexive frames.
explanations.notes_quote: Valid in all reflexive frames: Proof by contradiction. ...Since the frame is reflexive, $(w,w) \in R$, so from $\Box p$ we get $\mathfrak{M}, w \models p$

explanations.text: Reflexivity gives $(w,w) \in R$; applying the $\Box p$ condition to the self-loop yields $p$ at $w$.
explanations.notes_quote: Since the frame is reflexive, $(w,w) \in R$, so from $\Box p$ we get $\mathfrak{M}, w \models p$ — contradiction.

explanations.text: $A_T$ is not necessarily valid on transitive frames (which need not be reflexive). A counterexample: $W=\{a,b\}$, $R=\{(a,b),(b,b)\}$, $V(a,p)=0$, $V(b,p)=1$. Then $a$ has no self-loop, so $\Box p$ at $a$ but $p$ is false at $a$.

questions.text: The axiom $A_4 = \Diamond\Diamond p \to \Diamond p$ ('possibly possibly implies possibly') and transitive frames. Which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $A_4$ is not valid in all frames: a counterexample uses $W=\{a,b,c\}$, $R=\{(a,b),(b,c)\}$, $V(c,p)=1$
In the counterexample, $\mathfrak{M},a \models \Diamond\Diamond p$ (via $b$ then $c$) but $\mathfrak{M},a \not\models \Diamond p$ (only $b$ is accessible, $p$ false at $b$)
$A_4$ is valid in all transitive frames

Proof of validity on transitive frames: if $\Diamond\Diamond p$ at $w$, get $u$ with $(w,u)\in R$ and $\Diamond p$ at $u$, get $v$ with $(u,v)\in R$ and $p$ at $v$; transitivity gives $(w,v)\in R$ so $\Diamond p$ at $w$

explanations.text: The frame $\{a,b,c\}$ with $R=\{(a,b),(b,c)\}$ is a counterexample to $A_4$ for non-transitive frames.
explanations.notes_quote: Take $W = \{a,b,c\}$, $R = \{(a,b),(b,c)\}$, $V(c,p)=1$, all others 0

explanations.text: In the counterexample, $a$ can reach $c$ (satisfying $p$) via two steps, but not directly.
explanations.notes_quote: Then $\mathfrak{M}, a \models \Diamond\Diamond p$ (via $b$ then $c$), but $\mathfrak{M}, a \not\models \Diamond p$ (only $b$ accessible and $p$ is false there).

explanations.text: $A_4$ characterises exactly the class of transitive frames.
explanations.notes_quote: Valid in all transitive frames

explanations.text: Transitivity closes the two-step path into a direct step, yielding $\Diamond p$ at $w$.
explanations.notes_quote: By transitivity, $(w,v)\in R$, so $\mathfrak{M}, w \models \Diamond p$ — contradicts $\mathfrak{M}, w \not\models \Diamond p$.

questions.text: The correspondence between frame properties and modal axioms is a key theme. Which of the following correctly match frame properties with their axioms? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Reflexive frames ($\forall x: (x,x)\in R$) correspond to axiom $A_T: \Box p \to p$
Transitive frames ($(x,y),(y,z)\in R \Rightarrow (x,z)\in R$) correspond to axiom $A_4: \Diamond\Diamond p \to \Diamond p$
Symmetric frames ($(x,y)\in R \Rightarrow (y,x)\in R$) correspond to axiom $A_T$
All Kripke frames (with no additional properties) correspond to minimal logic $\mathbf{K}$ with axiom $\Box(p \to q) \to (\Box p \to \Box q)$

explanations.text: $A_T: \Box p \to p$ is valid iff the frame is reflexive.
explanations.notes_quote: $A_T$ (T): $\Box p \to p$ — Frame Property: Reflexive: $(x,x) \in R$ for all $x$

explanations.text: $A_4: \Diamond\Diamond p \to \Diamond p$ is valid iff the frame is transitive.
explanations.notes_quote: $A_4$ (4): $\Diamond\Diamond p \to \Diamond p$ — Frame Property: Transitive: $(x,y),(y,z)\in R \Rightarrow (x,z)\in R$

explanations.text: Symmetric frames correspond to axiom $B: p \to \Box\Diamond p$, not $A_T$.
Logic $\mathbf{K}$ (with axiom $A_K$) corresponds to all Kripke frames without additional constraints.
explanations.notes_quote: This is valid in all Kripke frames.

questions.text: The Labelling Algorithm computes $W(H)$ for a formula $H$ in a Kripke model. Which of the following correctly describe the algorithm? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The algorithm proceeds bottom-up, processing subformulas in order of increasing size
$\Box F$ is pre-processed to $\neg\Diamond\neg F$ before the algorithm runs
$F \to G$ is pre-processed to $\neg F \vee G$
To check validity over $\mathfrak{M}$, we check whether $W(H) = W$
The algorithm runs top-down, starting from $H$ and decomposing it

explanations.text: The divide-and-conquer strategy processes smaller subformulas first.
explanations.notes_quote: Use divide-and-conquer on subformulas, processing them in order of increasing size (bottom-up).

explanations.text: $\Box F$ is rewritten as $\neg\Diamond\neg F$ so only $\Diamond$ needs a direct case.
explanations.notes_quote: $\Box F \mapsto \neg\Diamond\neg F$

explanations.text: Implication is eliminated via $F \to G \mapsto \neg F \vee G$.
explanations.notes_quote: $F \to G \mapsto (\neg F \vee G)$

explanations.text: Model validity holds iff every world is in $W(H)$, i.e. $W(H) = W$.
explanations.notes_quote: To check validity over $\mathfrak{M}$: check whether $W(H) = W$.

explanations.text: The algorithm is bottom-up, not top-down. It builds up from atomic formulas.

questions.text: In the Labelling Algorithm, the case $H = \Diamond F$ is computed as follows. Which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $W(\Diamond F) = \{w \in W : \exists v \in W(F)$ such that $(w,v) \in R\}$
$W(\Diamond F)$ consists of all worlds that have at least one $R$-successor in $W(F)$
$W(\Box F) = \neg\Diamond\neg F$ is computed as $W \setminus W(\Diamond(W \setminus W(F)))$
$W(\Diamond F) = W(F)$ (Diamond is the identity operator)

explanations.text: A world $w$ is in $W(\Diamond F)$ iff it can see some world where $F$ holds.
explanations.notes_quote: $W(\Diamond F) = \{w \in W : (w, v) \in R \text{ for some } v \in W(F)\}$

explanations.text: Equivalently, $w \in W(\Diamond F)$ iff $w$ has an $R$-successor in $W(F)$.
explanations.notes_quote: Worlds that can 'see' some world in $W(F)$.

explanations.text:
Since $\Box F = 
eg\Diamond
eg F$: $W(\Box F) = W \setminus W(\Diamond
eg F) = W \setminus W(\Diamond(W \setminus W(F)))$.
explanations.notes_quote: After pre-processing, $\Box F$ becomes $\neg\Diamond\neg F$, handled by Cases 6 and 2.

explanations.text: $\Diamond$ is not the identity: $\Diamond F$ holds at $w$ based on what happens at successors of $w$, not $w$ itself.

questions.text: In SGT 9.1, using the model with $W = \{a,b,c,d,e\}$, $W(p) = \{a,b,c\}$, $W(q) = \{b,d\}$, and the given accessibility relation, compute $W(\Box(p \vee \neg q))$. Which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $W(\neg q) = \{a, c, e\}$
$W(p \vee \neg q) = \{a, b, c, e\}$
$W(\Box(p \vee \neg q)) = \{a, b, d, e\}$
World $c$ is excluded from $W(\Box(p \vee \neg q))$ because $c$'s only successor $d$ is not in $W(p \vee \neg q)$
$W(\Box(p \vee \neg q)) = \{a, b, c, d, e\}$ (valid over the model)

explanations.text: $W(\neg q) = W \setminus W(q) = \{a,b,c,d,e\} \setminus \{b,d\} = \{a,c,e\}$.
explanations.notes_quote: $W(\neg q) = \{a, c, e\}$

explanations.text: $W(p \vee \neg q) = W(p) \cup W(\neg q) = \{a,b,c\} \cup \{a,c,e\} = \{a,b,c,e\}$.
explanations.notes_quote: $W(p \vee \neg q) = \{a, b, c, e\}$

explanations.text: A world is in $W(\Box(p \vee \neg q))$ iff all its successors are in $\{a,b,c,e\}$.
explanations.notes_quote: Answer: $W(\Box(p \vee \neg q)) = \{a, b, d, e\}$

explanations.text:
$c$'s only successor is $d$, and $d 
otin \{a,b,c,e\}$, so $c 
otin W(\Box(p \vee 
eg q))$.
explanations.notes_quote:
World $c$ has successor $d$, and $d 
otin \{a,b,c,e\}$, so $c 
otin W(\Box(p\vee
eg q))$.

explanations.text: $W(\Box(p \vee \neg q)) = \{a,b,d,e\} \neq W$, so the formula is not valid over the model.

questions.text: Still using the SGT 9.1 model, compute $W(\Box\Diamond p)$. Which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $W(\Diamond p) = \{a, d\}$ because $a$ sees worlds in $W(p) = \{a,b,c\}$ and $d$ sees $c \in W(p)$
$W(\Box\Diamond p) = \{b, c\}$
$b \in W(\Box\Diamond p)$ because $b$ has no successors, making $\Box$ vacuously true
$a \in W(\Box\Diamond p)$ because $a$'s successor $e$ satisfies $\Diamond p$
$c \in W(\Box\Diamond p)$ because $c$'s only successor $d$ is in $W(\Diamond p)$

explanations.text: $W(\Diamond p)$: $a$ can see $b,c,a \in W(p)$ — yes; $d$ can see $c \in W(p)$ — yes; $b$ has no successors — no; $c$ sees only $d \notin W(p)$ — no; $e$ sees only $e \notin W(p)$ — no.
explanations.notes_quote: $W(\Diamond p) = \{a, d\}$

explanations.text: $W(\Box\Diamond p) = \{b, c\}$ as computed in the solutions.
explanations.notes_quote: $W(\Box\Diamond p) = \{b, c\}$

explanations.text: With no successors, the condition 'all successors are in $W(\Diamond p)$' is vacuously true.
explanations.notes_quote: $b$ has no successors so $\Box$ is vacuously true

explanations.text:
$a$ has successor $e$, and $e 
otin W(\Diamond p)$ (since $e$ sees only $e 
otin W(p)$). So $a 
otin W(\Box\Diamond p)$.
explanations.notes_quote: World $a$ has successor $e$, $e \notin W(\Diamond p).

explanations.text: $c$ has only successor $d \in W(\Diamond p) = \{a,d\}$, so $c \in W(\Box\Diamond p)$.
explanations.notes_quote: $c$ has only successor $d$, and $d \in W(\Diamond p)$.

questions.text: SGT 9.2 asks for formulas $F_w$ such that $W(F_w) = \{w\}$. Which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $F_b = \Box\bot$ (or equivalently $p \wedge q$ given the valuation) satisfies $W(F_b) = \{b\}$
$F_a = \Diamond(\Box p \wedge \Box\neg q)$ satisfies $W(F_a) = \{a\}$
$F_e = \neg q \wedge \Box\neg q$ is a formula satisfied only at $e$
It is always possible to distinguish every world in any Kripke model by a modal formula

explanations.text: $\Box\bot$ is true iff there are no successors — only $b$ has no successors. Alternatively $p \wedge q$ holds only at $b$ given the valuation.
explanations.notes_quote: $w = b$: $F_b = (p \wedge q)$, or $F_b = \Box\bot$ where $\bot$ is a propositional contradiction

explanations.text: From SGT 9.1(iii), $W(\Diamond(\Box p \wedge \Box\neg q)) = \{a\}$.
explanations.notes_quote: $w = a$: $F_a = \Diamond(\Box p \wedge \Box\neg q)$

explanations.text: $e$'s only successor is $e$ itself, and $\neg q$ holds at $e$, so both conditions are met uniquely at $e$.
explanations.notes_quote: $w = e$: $F_e = \neg q \wedge \Box\neg q$

explanations.text: In general, not every model has modally distinguishable worlds. Bisimilar worlds satisfy exactly the same modal formulas and cannot be distinguished.

questions.text: SGT 9.3 proves that $\Diamond\neg p \leftrightarrow \neg\Box p$ is valid in all Kripke frames. The proof proceeds by contradiction. Which of the following correctly describe the proof? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Case 1: If $\mathfrak{M},w \models \Diamond\neg p$ and $\mathfrak{M},w \models \Box p$, then some accessible world has both $p$ true and $p$ false — contradiction

Case 2: If $\mathfrak{M},w 
ot\models \Diamond
eg p$ and $\mathfrak{M},w \models 
eg\Box p$, then some accessible world satisfies $
eg p$, giving $\Diamond
eg p$ — contradiction
The equivalence $\Diamond\neg p \leftrightarrow \neg\Box p$ expresses a duality between $\Diamond$ and $\Box$
The proof requires the frame to be reflexive

explanations.text: From $\Diamond\neg p$: some $u$ with $(w,u)\in R$ and $V(u,p)=0$. From $\Box p$: all such $u$ have $V(u,p)=1$. Contradiction.
explanations.notes_quote:
Case 1: From $\Diamond
eg p$: $\exists u$ with $(w,u)\in R$ and $\mathfrak{M}, u \models 
eg p$ ... From $
ot\models 
eg\Box p$: $\mathfrak{M}, w \models \Box p$ ... Contradiction

explanations.text:
From $\models 
eg\Box p$: some $u$ with $(w,u)\in R$ and $p$ false at $u$, giving $\Diamond
eg p$ at $w$, contradicting $
ot\models \Diamond
eg p$.
explanations.notes_quote:
Case 2: From $\models 
eg\Box p$: $\mathfrak{M}, w 
ot\models \Box p$, so $\exists u$ with $(w,u)\in R$ and $\mathfrak{M}, u 
ot\models p$ ... But then $\mathfrak{M}, w \models \Diamond
eg p$

explanations.text: $\Diamond\neg p \leftrightarrow \neg\Box p$ is the standard $\Diamond/\Box$ duality, holding in all Kripke frames.
The proof requires no frame properties — it holds in all Kripke frames (including non-reflexive ones).
explanations.notes_quote: Claim: $A = \Diamond\neg p \leftrightarrow \neg\Box p$ is valid in all Kripke frames.

questions.source_ref: Section 9.6: SGT 9.4 — A_T and Reflexive Frames
questions.text: SGT 9.4 asks to give a counter-example showing $A_T = \Box p \to p$ is not valid in all frames, and then prove it valid in reflexive frames. Which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A minimal counterexample: $W=\{a\}$, $R=\varnothing$, $V(a,p)=0$. Then $\Box p$ is vacuously true but $p$ is false.
Another counterexample: $W=\{a,b\}$, $R=\{(a,b)\}$, $V(a,p)=0$, $V(b,p)=1$. Then $\mathfrak{M},a \models \Box p$ but $\mathfrak{M},a \not\models p$.

Validity on reflexive frames is proved by contradiction: assume $\mathfrak{M},w \models \Box p$ and $\mathfrak{M},w \not\models p$, then reflexivity gives $(w,w)\in R$, so $p$ must hold at $w$ — contradiction.
Validity on reflexive frames requires transitivity, not just reflexivity.

explanations.text: With $R=\varnothing$ and $V(a,p)=0$: $\Box p$ at $a$ (vacuously) but $p$ false at $a$.
explanations.notes_quote: Minimal: $W = \{a\}$, $R = \emptyset$, $V(a,p)=0$. Then $\Box p$ is vacuously true (no successors) but $p$ is false

explanations.text: $a$ has only successor $b$ where $p$ holds, so $\mathfrak{M},a \models \Box p$, but $p$ is false at $a$ itself.
explanations.notes_quote: Slightly larger: $W = \{a,b\}$, $R = \{(a,b)\}$, $V(a,p)=0$, $V(b,p)=1$. Then $\mathfrak{M}, a \models \Box p$ but $\mathfrak{M}, a \not\models p$.

explanations.text: Reflexivity gives $(w,w)\in R$; since $\Box p$ holds, $p$ must hold at $w$ — contradicting $\not\models p$.
explanations.notes_quote: Proof by contradiction: suppose $\mathfrak{M}, w \not\models \Box p \to p$ on a reflexive frame. Then $\mathfrak{M}, w \models \Box p$ (1) and $\mathfrak{M}, w \not\models p$ (2). Since the frame is reflexive: $(w,w) \in R$. From (1), $\mathfrak{M}, w \models p$. Contradicts (2).

explanations.text: $A_T$ validity requires only reflexivity, not transitivity.

questions.source_ref: Section 9.6: SGT 9.5 — A_4 and Transitive Frames
questions.text: SGT 9.5 concerns $A_4 = \Diamond\Diamond p \to \Diamond p$. Which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Counterexample: $W=\{a,b,c\}$, $R=\{(a,b),(b,c)\}$, $V(c,p)=1$, others 0. $A_4$ fails at $a$.

On a transitive frame: from $\Diamond\Diamond p$ at $w$, get $u$ with $(w,u)\in R$ and $\Diamond p$ at $u$; get $v$ with $(u,v)\in R$ and $p$ at $v$; transitivity gives $(w,v)\in R$, so $\Diamond p$ at $w$
The proof of $A_4$ on transitive frames uses proof by contradiction
$A_4$ holds on all Kripke frames, not just transitive ones

explanations.text: $a$ reaches $c$ (where $p$ holds) via two hops but not directly. So $\Diamond\Diamond p$ at $a$ but $\Diamond p$ fails.
explanations.notes_quote: $W = \{a,b,c\}$, $R = \{(a,b),(b,c)\}$, $V(a,p)=V(b,p)=0$, $V(c,p)=1$. Then $\mathfrak{M}, a \models \Diamond\Diamond p$ (reach $c$ via $b$), but $\mathfrak{M}, a \not\models \Diamond p$

explanations.text: Transitivity shortcuts $(w,u),(u,v)\in R$ to $(w,v)\in R$, giving $\Diamond p$ at $w$.
explanations.notes_quote: By transitivity: $(w,u),(u,v)\in R \Rightarrow (w,v)\in R$. So $\mathfrak{M}, w \models \Diamond p$

explanations.text: The proof supposes $\Diamond\Diamond p$ at $w$ but $\not\models \Diamond p$, then derives $\Diamond p$ via transitivity — a contradiction.
explanations.notes_quote: Proof by contradiction: suppose $\mathfrak{M}, w \models \Diamond\Diamond p$ (16) but $\mathfrak{M}, w \not\models \Diamond p$ (17) on a transitive frame.

explanations.text: $A_4$ fails on non-transitive frames (as the counterexample shows). It is valid exactly on transitive frames.

questions.source_ref: Key Definitions: Kripke Model vs Frame
questions.text: Which of the following correctly distinguish a Kripke model from a Kripke frame? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A Kripke model $\mathfrak{M} = \langle W, R, V \rangle$ includes a valuation $V$; a Kripke frame $\mathfrak{F} = \langle W, R \rangle$ does not
Frame validity ($\mathfrak{F} \models F$) is stronger than model validity ($\mathfrak{M} \models F$) because it quantifies over all valuations
A frame and a model have the same number of worlds $W$
Validity over a class of frames means the formula holds for every frame in that class under every valuation

explanations.text: The frame is the structural component (worlds + accessibility); the model adds propositional truth assignments.
explanations.notes_quote: Kripke model: A triple $\mathfrak{M} = \langle W, R, V \rangle$: worlds, accessibility relation, valuation; Kripke frame: A pair $\mathfrak{F} = \langle W, R \rangle$: worlds and accessibility relation (no valuation)

explanations.text: Frame validity universally quantifies over both worlds and valuations; model validity only quantifies over worlds.
explanations.notes_quote: $\mathfrak{F} \models F$: fix frame; universal over worlds and valuations

explanations.text: While the same $W$ underlies both, this is trivially true. The key distinction is the presence or absence of $V$.
Validity over a class of frames: $F$ valid in every frame from the class under every valuation.
explanations.notes_quote: $F$ is valid over a class of frames $\mathcal{C}$ if $\mathfrak{F} \models F$ for every $\mathfrak{F} \in \mathcal{C}$

questions.source_ref: Key Definitions: Accessibility Relation and Possible Worlds
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the concept of possible worlds and the accessibility relation in Kripke semantics? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $(w, u) \in R$ means 'world $u$ is accessible from world $w$'
To evaluate $\Diamond F$ at $w$, we look at all successors of $w$ in $R$
Possible worlds are vertices of a directed graph, with accessibility edges
The accessibility relation must be an equivalence relation in all Kripke models
A possible world $w$ has its own propositional valuation, so different worlds can assign different truth values to the same proposition

explanations.text: $(w,u) \in R$ represents the accessibility direction from $w$ to $u$.
explanations.notes_quote: Accessibility relation: $R \subseteq W \times W$; $(w,u)\in R$ means '$u$ is accessible from $w$'

explanations.text: $\Diamond F$ is true at $w$ iff $F$ holds at some $R$-successor of $w$.
explanations.notes_quote: $\Diamond F$ is true at $w$ iff $F$ is true at some accessible world

explanations.text: A Kripke model has the structure of a directed graph with labelled vertices.
explanations.notes_quote: Intuition: $W$ is a directed graph of possible worlds. $(w, u) \in R$ means 'world $u$ is accessible from world $w$'.

explanations.text: The accessibility relation in a general Kripke model (logic $\mathbf{K}$) has no required properties. Equivalence relations correspond to modal logic $\mathbf{S5}$.
Each world $w$ has its own valuation $V(w, \cdot)$, allowing different truth assignments per world.
explanations.notes_quote: Possible world: A node $w \in W$ representing a state of affairs with its own propositional valuation

questions.text: Which of the following statements about the minimal modal logic $\mathbf{K}$ are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\mathbf{K}$ is the set of all modal formulas valid in every Kripke frame
Axiom $A_K = \Box(p \to q) \to (\Box p \to \Box q)$ is a tautology of $\mathbf{K}$
The Completeness Theorem for $\mathbf{K}$: semantic consequence equals provability ($\Gamma \models^\mathbf{K} F \iff \Gamma \vdash^\mathbf{K} F$)
$\mathbf{K}$ contains axiom $A_T$ ($\Box p \to p$) as a tautology valid in all frames
Logics $\mathbf{T}$ and $\mathbf{K4}$ are extensions of $\mathbf{K}$ obtained by restricting to reflexive and transitive frames respectively

explanations.text: $\mathbf{K}$ collects all universal validities over all Kripke frames.
explanations.notes_quote: Minimal modal logic K: The set of all modal formulas valid in all Kripke frames

explanations.text: $A_K$ is valid in all Kripke frames and is thus in $\mathbf{K}$.
explanations.notes_quote: Important tautology (Axiom K): $\Box(p \to q) \to (\Box p \to \Box q)$. This is valid in all Kripke frames.

explanations.text: The Completeness Theorem for $\mathbf{K}$ states that semantic and syntactic consequence coincide.
explanations.notes_quote: Theorem 9.6 (Completeness of K): $\Gamma \models^{\mathbf{K}} F \iff \Gamma \vdash^{\mathbf{k}} F$

explanations.text: $A_T = \Box p \to p$ is NOT a tautology of $\mathbf{K}$; it fails in non-reflexive frames. It belongs to the stronger logic $\mathbf{T}$.
explanations.notes_quote: Not valid in all frames

explanations.text: Logic $\mathbf{T}$ extends $\mathbf{K}$ with $A_T$ (reflexive frames); $\mathbf{K4}$ extends $\mathbf{K}$ with $A_4$ (transitive frames).

questions.source_ref: Key Definitions: Model-Checking and Labelling Algorithm
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe model-checking and the Labelling Algorithm? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Model-checking is the problem: given $\mathfrak{M}$, $w$, and $F$, does $\mathfrak{M}, w \models F$?
The Labelling Algorithm computes $W(F)$ bottom-up by processing subformulas from smallest to largest

Pre-processing rewrites $\Box F$ as $
eg\Diamond
eg F$ and $F 	o G$ as $
eg F \vee G$
The algorithm runs in exponential time in the number of worlds
To check if $\mathfrak{M} \models F$ (model validity), check that $W(F) = W$

explanations.text: Model-checking determines truth of a formula at a specified world in a given model.
explanations.notes_quote: Model-checking: Determining whether $\mathfrak{M}, w \models F$ for a given model, world, and formula

explanations.text: The divide-and-conquer strategy works bottom-up on the formula structure.
explanations.notes_quote: Labelling Algorithm: Bottom-up algorithm computing $W(F)$ by processing subformulas in order of size

explanations.text: Pre-processing eliminates $\Box$ and $\to$, reducing to $\neg$, $\vee$, $\wedge$, $\Diamond$.
explanations.notes_quote:
Pre-processing step: Rewrite $H$ using these equivalences to eliminate $	o$ and $\Box$: $F 	o G \mapsto (
eg F \vee G)$ and $\Box F \mapsto 
eg\Diamond
eg F$

explanations.text: The algorithm runs in polynomial time (linear in model size times formula size), not exponential.
Model validity means $F$ holds at every world, i.e. $W(F) = W$.
explanations.notes_quote: To check validity over $\mathfrak{M}$: check whether $W(H) = W$.

questions.source_ref: Key Definitions: Summary of Frame Properties
questions.text: Which of the following correctly summarise key frame properties and their modal significance? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A reflexive frame has $(w,w) \in R$ for all $w \in W$
A transitive frame has: if $(x,y) \in R$ and $(y,z) \in R$ then $(x,z) \in R$
Reflexive + transitive + symmetric frames correspond to modal logic $\mathbf{S5}$
Transitive frames satisfy $A_T = \Box p \to p$
Modal logic $\mathbf{T}$ is the logic of reflexive frames and is strictly stronger than $\mathbf{K}$

explanations.text: A reflexive frame has every world accessible to itself.
explanations.notes_quote: Reflexive frame: A frame where $(x,x)\in R$ for all $x\in W$

explanations.text: Transitivity closes two-step paths into direct connections.
explanations.notes_quote: Transitive frame: A frame where $(x,y),(y,z)\in R \Rightarrow (x,z)\in R$

explanations.text: Logic $\mathbf{S5}$ is complete with respect to equivalence relations (reflexive + symmetric + transitive).
Transitive frames satisfy $A_4 = \Diamond\Diamond p \to \Diamond p$, not $A_T$. The counterexample to $A_T$ uses a non-reflexive (but trivially transitive) frame.
$\mathbf{T}$ extends $\mathbf{K}$ by adding $A_T$, making it strictly stronger (more tautologies, fewer valid frames).

questions.text: Modal operators are described as 'non-truth-functional'. Which of the following correctly explain this? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The truth value of $\Diamond F$ at $w$ is not determined solely by the truth value of $F$ at $w$
Whether $\Diamond F$ is true at $w$ depends on what happens at other accessible worlds
There are only four possible truth tables for a unary connective on $\{0,1\}$, and none captures 'possibly'
$\Diamond$ is non-truth-functional because it has three possible output values: 0, $\frac{1}{2}$, and 1

explanations.text: Even if $F$ is false at $w$, $\Diamond F$ may be true at $w$ if $F$ holds at some accessible world.
explanations.notes_quote: Non-truth-functional: An operator whose value is not determined solely by the truth value of its argument

explanations.text: Possibility depends on accessible worlds — a global/relational feature not captured by local truth tables.
explanations.notes_quote: Modal operators are non-truth-functional: the truth value of $\Diamond F$ is not determined solely by the truth value of $F$

explanations.text: None of the four unary truth tables (identity, negation, constant 0, constant 1) captures possibility.
explanations.notes_quote: There are only four possible truth tables for a unary connective; none captures 'possibly' or 'necessarily'

explanations.text: Classical Kripke semantics for $\Diamond$ is two-valued (0 or 1). Three values relate to fuzzy/many-valued logic, not modal logic.

questions.text: The satisfaction clauses for classical connectives in modal logic use the classical two-valued semantics. Which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $V(w, \neg F) = 1 - V(w, F)$
$V(w, F \wedge G) = \min(V(w,F), V(w,G))$
$V(w, F \to G) = \max(1 - V(w,F), V(w,G))$
$V(w, F \vee G) = V(w,F) \cdot V(w,G)$
$V(w, \Box F) = \min\{V(u,F) : (w,u) \in R\}$

explanations.text: Negation flips truth: $V(w, \neg F) = 1 - V(w, F)$.
explanations.notes_quote: $V(w, \neg F) = 1 - V(w, F)$

explanations.text: Conjunction is the minimum of the two values (two-valued = classical and).
explanations.notes_quote: $V(w, F \wedge G) = \min(V(w, F),\ V(w, G))$

explanations.text: Implication: $\max(1-V(w,F), V(w,G))$ is the classical two-valued implication.
explanations.notes_quote: $V(w, F \to G) = \max(1 - V(w, F),\ V(w, G))$

explanations.text: $V(w, F \vee G) = \max(V(w,F), V(w,G))$, not the product.
explanations.notes_quote: $V(w, F \vee G) = \max(V(w, F),\ V(w, G))$

explanations.text: $\Box F$ is true iff $F$ is true at all successors — the minimum over successors.
explanations.notes_quote: $V(w, \Box F) = \min\{V(u, F) : (w, u) \in R\}$

questions.source_ref: Key Definitions: Completeness and Logics K, T, K4
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the relationship between logics $\mathbf{K}$, $\mathbf{T}$, and $\mathbf{K4}$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\mathbf{K} \subseteq \mathbf{T}$: every tautology of $\mathbf{K}$ is also a tautology of $\mathbf{T}$
$\mathbf{T}$ is obtained from $\mathbf{K}$ by adding $A_T = \Box p \to p$ and restricting to reflexive frames
$\mathbf{K4}$ is obtained from $\mathbf{K}$ by adding $A_4 = \Diamond\Diamond p \to \Diamond p$ and restricting to transitive frames
$\mathbf{K}$, $\mathbf{T}$, and $\mathbf{K4}$ are all complete (semantics coincides with proof system)
$\mathbf{T}$ and $\mathbf{K4}$ are incomparable: neither is a subset of the other

explanations.text: $\mathbf{T}$ adds new axioms to $\mathbf{K}$, so $\mathbf{K} \subseteq \mathbf{T}$ (every $\mathbf{K}$-valid formula is $\mathbf{T}$-valid).
Logic $\mathbf{T}$ is $\mathbf{K}$ plus $A_T$, valid on reflexive frames.
explanations.notes_quote: $A_T$ (T): $\Box p \to p$ — Frame Property: Reflexive

explanations.text: Logic $\mathbf{K4}$ is $\mathbf{K}$ plus $A_4$, valid on transitive frames.
explanations.notes_quote: $A_4$ (4): $\Diamond\Diamond p \to \Diamond p$ — Frame Property: Transitive

explanations.text: All three logics have completeness theorems relating semantics to proof systems.
explanations.notes_quote: Theorem 9.6 (Completeness of K)

explanations.text: $\mathbf{T}$ and $\mathbf{K4}$ are indeed incomparable in the general case (neither is contained in the other), but the statement's tone implies a definitive structural claim beyond what is in the notes. The fact that $A_T \notin \mathbf{K4}$ (non-reflexive transitive frames) and $A_4 \notin \mathbf{T}$ (reflexive non-transitive frames) shows they are incomparable, which is correct. However this fact is not explicitly stated in the week 9 notes, so it should not be selected.

questions.source_ref: Section 9.3.2: Empty Successors and Vacuous Truth
questions.text: Consider a world $w$ in a Kripke model with no successors (i.e. no $u$ with $(w,u) \in R$). Which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\mathfrak{M}, w \models \Box F$ for every formula $F$ (vacuously true)
$\mathfrak{M}, w \not\models \Diamond F$ for every formula $F$ (vacuously false)
$\mathfrak{M}, w \models \Box\bot$ where $\bot$ is a contradiction (even though $\bot$ is never true)
$\mathfrak{M}, w \models \Diamond F$ for every formula $F$ (vacuously true)

explanations.text: The universal condition for $\Box F$ ('all successors satisfy $F$') is vacuously true when there are no successors.
explanations.notes_quote: If $w$ has no successors (no $(w, u) \in R$), then $\Box F$ is vacuously true

explanations.text: The existential condition for $\Diamond F$ ('some successor satisfies $F$') cannot be met with no successors.
explanations.notes_quote: If $w$ has no successors (no $(w, u) \in R$), then $\Diamond F$ is vacuously false at $w$.

explanations.text: $\Box\bot$ requires all successors to satisfy $\bot$; with no successors this is vacuously true. This is exploited in SGT 9.2 to identify worlds with no successors.
explanations.notes_quote: $F_b = \Box\bot$ where $\bot$ is a propositional contradiction (since $b$ has no successors, $\Box\bot$ is vacuously true only at $b$)

explanations.text: $\Diamond F$ is vacuously false (not true) when there are no successors: the existential has no witness.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly summarise the key results and definitions from Week 9? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Kripke semantics was introduced in 1963 by Saul Kripke to give models for modal logic
The key insight for $\Diamond$: truth at a world depends on truth at accessible worlds, not just the current world
Modal logic $\mathbf{K}$ is the weakest normal modal logic, valid on all Kripke frames
The Labelling Algorithm checks model validity by computing $W(F)$ bottom-up and verifying $W(F) = W$
$A_T$ and $A_4$ are both tautologies of $\mathbf{K}$ (valid in all frames)

explanations.text: Saul Kripke introduced possible-worlds semantics for modal logic in 1963.
explanations.notes_quote: Introduced in 1963 by Saul Kripke.

explanations.text: $\Diamond F$ at $w$ is determined by $F$'s truth at $w$'s $R$-successors — a relational, not purely local, feature.
explanations.notes_quote: $\Diamond F$ is true at $w$ iff $F$ is true at some accessible world

explanations.text: $\mathbf{K}$ is the minimal modal logic: valid in all Kripke frames, the foundation for stronger logics.
explanations.notes_quote: Minimal modal logic K: The set of all modal formulas valid in all Kripke frames

explanations.text: The Labelling Algorithm computes $W(F)$ and checks if $W(F) = W$ for model validity.
explanations.notes_quote: To check validity over $\mathfrak{M}$: check whether $W(H) = W$.

explanations.text: $A_T = \Box p \to p$ is NOT a tautology of $\mathbf{K}$ (fails on non-reflexive frames); $A_4 = \Diamond\Diamond p \to \Diamond p$ is NOT a tautology of $\mathbf{K}$ (fails on non-transitive frames). They are tautologies of $\mathbf{T}$ and $\mathbf{K4}$ respectively.
explanations.notes_quote: Not valid in all frames
